body: most of my development is done on my local machine, which chokes on datasets that most people wouldn't even think of as big data . one of the reasons that i was drawn to drake in the first place was the idea that i could segment my data, and let my machine work on each part individually, making checkpoints along the way so that if it does choke, i don't have to re-run the whole thing. so i wrote a series of functions that give me similar functionality to dplyr::do , but in drake. specifically, i've written drake_split and drake_unsplit commands, which output plans. this allows me to ~~horribly misuse~~ creatively exploit the analyses function to process my small data chunks in parallel, including tasks such as validating using assertr and cleaning. i've got the bones of this issue solved, but i don't want to turn it into a pr until i can write some tests and maybe a vignette or example hopefully later this week . mostly, i'm opening this to solicit requests for functionality and to put it on the roadmap for later milestones. current/planned features: - splits automatically: - x data.frame - x tibble - data.table it might do this, i haven't tested at all - x recombines automatically - x plays well with magrittr pipe - x plays well with multi-step pipechains ex: mtcars %>% head %>% drake_split - x respects dplyr groups - x attempts to distribute groups evenly by row count among slices - x can apply multiple steps of analysis to each parallel slice i use this to validate, clean, and then analyze each slice - offer a choice of using the dplyr of base functions for splitting/binding - documentation - roxygen docs - example script - vignette - unit tests for each feature

claims.claim: extras semantics should be better defined.
claims.Evidence_quote: extras semantics should be better defined.

claims.claim: it is possible to define the same property in extras that belong to different original properties.
claims.Evidence_quote: it is possible to define the same property in extras that belong to different original properties.

claims.claim: it is even possible to define the same property more than once in the same extras array.
claims.Evidence_quote: it is even possible to define the same property more than once in the same extras array.

claims.claim: what is the protocol here?
claims.Evidence_quote: what is the protocol here?

claims.claim: examples: a decorator wants to provide a default implementation for a certain method, if it is not defined by a user or some other library.
claims.Evidence_quote: examples: a decorator wants to provide a default implementation for a certain method, if it is not defined by a user or some other library.

claims.claim: or piggy-back on it.
claims.Evidence_quote: or piggy-back on it.

claims.claim: i want to override destroy method in different decorators, so i can destroy/finalize objects created to fulfill decorators purpose
claims.Evidence_quote: i want to override destroy method in different decorators, so i can destroy/finalize objects created to fulfill decorators purpose

claims.claim: delete html elements, remove event listeners, flush streams, notify a server that i am done, and so on
claims.Evidence_quote: delete html elements, remove event listeners, flush streams, notify a server that i am done, and so on

claims.claim: ideally it would be nice to have a way to reconcile such duplicates with a callback,
claims.Evidence_quote: ideally it would be nice to have a way to reconcile such duplicates with a callback,

claims.claim: and provide a reasonable default for it.
claims.Evidence_quote: and provide a reasonable default for it.

claims.claim: finisher looks like the right place for that.
claims.Evidence_quote: finisher looks like the right place for that.

claims.claim: for that it should receive dups as a parameter, so potentially it can make sense of it.
claims.Evidence_quote: for that it should receive dups as a parameter, so potentially it can make sense of it.

claims.claim: the default action can be as simple as overwriting duplicate properties indiscriminately.
claims.Evidence_quote: the default action can be as simple as overwriting duplicate properties indiscriminately.

claims.claim: just for completeness: if it is possible to add more properties, why it is not possible to remove some of them?
claims.Evidence_quote: just for completeness: if it is possible to add more properties, why it is not possible to remove some of them?

claims.claim: the simplest example would be a meta-property, which is saved to guide other decorators, or reflection methods, yet should not be preserved on a property/instance/constructor.
claims.Evidence_quote: the simplest example would be a meta-property, which is saved to guide other decorators, or reflection methods, yet should not be preserved on a property/instance/constructor.

body: extras semantics should be better defined. right now it is possible to define the same property in extras that belong to different original properties. it is even possible to define the same property more than once in the same extras array. what is the protocol here? while it may indicate a bug, if somebody did it inadvertently, i suspect that the use case for that can be third-party libraries, which intentionally decided to create a property with the same name. examples: a decorator wants to provide a default implementation for a certain method, if it is not defined by a user or some other library. or piggy-back on it. i want to override destroy method in different decorators, so i can destroy/finalize objects created to fulfill decorators purpose delete html elements, remove event listeners, flush streams, notify a server that i am done, and so on . ideally it would be nice to have a way to reconcile such duplicates with a callback, and provide a reasonable default for it. finisher looks like the right place for that. for that it should receive dups as a parameter, so potentially it can make sense of it. the default action can be as simple as overwriting duplicate properties indiscriminately. just for completeness: if it is possible to add more properties, why it is not possible to remove some of them? the simplest example would be a meta-property, which is saved to guide other decorators, or reflection methods, yet should not be preserved on a property/instance/constructor.

claims.claim: Using == in JavaScript can lead to type coercion and unintended results.
claims.Evidence_quote: in almost all situations in javascript it is better to use === than == . == will coerce types and thus not give intended results sometimes.

claims.claim: 1 == 1 evaluates to true in JavaScript.
claims.Evidence_quote: eg: js 1 == 1 // true

claims.claim: 1 === 1 evaluates to false in JavaScript.
claims.Evidence_quote: eg: js 1 === 1 // false

claims.claim: JavaScript has a feature where 1 == 1 is true but 1 === 1 is false.
claims.Evidence_quote: eg: js 1 == 1 // true 1 === 1 // false

claims.claim: The triple equals operator (===) should be used in places of double equals (==) in JavaScript.
claims.Evidence_quote: i would heavily recommend changing any time you use == or != to === or !== respectively.

body: in almost all situations in javascript it is better to use === than == . == will coerce types and thus not give intended results sometimes. i would heavily recommend changing any time you use == or != to === or !== respectively. eg: js 1 == 1 // true 1 === 1 // false
